# Abstract

## Background

Major depressive disorder (MDD) is a leading cause of disability worldwide. Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is recommended as a first-line treatment, but the magnitude of its effects requires systematic evaluation.

## Objectives

To evaluate the effectiveness of CBT compared with treatment as usual (TAU) or waitlist control for adults with MDD.

## Methods

We searched PubMed, Scopus, and the Cochrane Library through December 2023. We included randomized controlled trials comparing CBT (individual, group, or online) with TAU or waitlist in adults with MDD. Two reviewers independently screened studies and extracted data. We used random-effects meta-analysis to pool standardized mean differences (SMD) for continuous outcomes and risk ratios (RR) for binary outcomes.

## Results

Seven RCTs (n=778) met inclusion criteria. CBT showed a moderate-to-large effect on depression severity measured by HDRS (SMD = -0.77; 95% CI: -0.92 to -0.61; k=5; I²=0%). Treatment response was significantly higher with CBT (RR = 1.52; 95% CI: 1.11 to 2.08; k=3). Results were consistent across delivery formats and comparator types.

## Conclusions

CBT is effective for reducing depression symptoms compared with TAU or waitlist control, with consistent effects across different delivery formats. The certainty of evidence is moderate, limited by risk of bias in some trials.

## Keywords

cognitive behavioral therapy, major depressive disorder, meta-analysis, systematic review, randomized controlled trial
